CAA Environmental Assessor reviews. If trial area has protected species (e.g., bat maternity roost within 1 km), applicant must obtain Natural England license. TBD: CAA publishes noise-modeling guidance (swarm aircraft signature modeling) and species-disturbance assessment template by 2026-Q3.

tbd_inventory.reason: REQ020 specifies lessons-learned workshop (30 days), technical debrief, and 2+ year evidence retention, but long-term learning capture across trial cohorts (institutional learning) is not yet systematized. Unclear how lessons from one trial inform future trials.
tbd_inventory.responsible: CAA Programme Manager + CAA Learning & Development team
tbd_inventory.current_placeholder: Odin rewrite specifies timeline (30-day workshop, 2-year retention) and close-out notice. But no systematic mechanism to extract lessons, codify, and apply to future trials. No CAA institutional learning process specified.
tbd_inventory.resolution_path: After first trial cohort completes (2026-Q4), CAA Programme Manager and Learning team design 'Cross-Trial Lessons-Learned Archive' (structured database of findings, root causes, recommendations, regulatory implications) and establish annual review cycle to update clause library based on lessons. TBD: CAA publishes post-trial assurance governance by 2026-Q4.

tbd_inventory.topic: regulatory clause library evolution and backward-compatibility mechanics
tbd_inventory.reason: REQ016 (phased gates) and GOAL-014 (regulatory library versioning) assume regulatory requirements evolve over time, but mechanics for versioning, pinning, and backward-compatibility are not specified. Unclear how in-flight cases handle mid-trial requirement changes.
tbd_inventory.responsible: CAA Regulatory Affairs + CAA IT (version control)
tbd_inventory.current_placeholder: Odin rewrite and Grace goals mention 'version pinning' and 'backward compatibility for in-flight cases,' but implementation not detailed. Example gap: if CAP 1616 engagement requirements change mid-trial, does ongoing trial have to comply with new version or continue under old?
tbd_inventory.resolution_path: CAA IT and Regulatory Affairs design 'Clause Library Version Management Policy' (Q2 2026) specifying: (1) version-release cadence (e.g., quarterly); (2) effective-date rules (new version applies to new cases, old version applies to in-flight cases unless applicant opts-in); (3) deprecated-requirement handling (can requirements be removed or only added?); (4) breaking-change escalation (if requirement change conflicts with active case, escalate to Programme Manager). Workbench must enforce version pinning per policy.

tbd_inventory.topic: panel override authority and appeal mechanism
tbd_inventory.reason: GOAL-015 specifies panel override capability with audit trail, but decision authority (who can override whom?) and appeal mechanism (can applicants contest overrides?) are not formalized.
tbd_inventory.responsible: CAA Director (policy authority) + CAA Programme Manager (decision authority)
tbd_inventory.current_placeholder: Odin rewrite and Grace goals mention override logging and justification recording, but no authorization matrix (director overrides chief inspector? vice versa?) or appeal process defined.
tbd_inventory.resolution_path: CAA Director issues 'Panel Decision Authority & Override Policy' (Q2 2026) specifying: (1) panel composition and voting authority; (2) override thresholds (e.g., director can override chief inspector on technical grounds with written justification); (3) appeal process (applicant can request director review of panel decision within 14 days); (4) escalation (if CAA and MAA panel members disagree on classified trial, escalate to executive level). Workbench must log all overrides and appeals.

completeness_metrics.reasoning: SRS has strong operational specification for 14 of 20 requirements (Odin rewrites) and comprehensive goal-based architecture (15 Grace goals). Chris glossary provides 42-58 canonical terms (sufficient for initial shared understanding). However, 20 TBDs span critical swarm-safety methodology gaps (swarm-aware SORA, emergent-behavior credibility, containment design standard, meaningful human control thresholds) and major governance gaps (insurance adequacy calculation, regulatory library evolution, panel authority formalization). No section is completely empty, but Section 3 (Specific Requirements) has 10 partially-specified REQs (REQ008-REQ011, REQ015-REQ020) requiring stakeholder workshops or per-trial negotiation. Recommendation: Proceed with trial intake using Odin-defined REQs and Grace goals; schedule TBD workshops (WS-001 swarm SORA, WS-002 containment design, WS-003 human factors) in Q2-Q3 2026 to close critical gaps before second trial cohort.

findings.finding_title: REQ017-020 features built on unvalidated stakeholder assumptions
findings.requirement: REQ017: Public and Stakeholder Engagement; REQ018: Security Classification Handling; REQ019: Environmental Impact; REQ020: Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out
findings.source_reference: Source REQ CSV rows 18-21; DeFOSPAM features F19-F22 (not explicit in user stories)
findings.reasoning: The source requirements explicitly state: 'No user story covers' these topics. Yet four features (F19-F22) address them. Features F19 (Public & Stakeholder Engagement), F20 (Security Classification Handling), F21 (Environmental Impact Assessment), F22 (Post-Trial Close-out) are implicitly derived from regulatory hints (CAP 1616, GDPR, JSP 440) and the system context, NOT from explicit CAA/MAA stakeholder approval. This violates the IEEE 830-1998 principle that requirements shall reflect agreed user needs, not analyst assumptions about what regulators might want. The SRS claims to derive from 20 explicit REQs, but introduces 4 features from absent requirements.
findings.recommendation: Obtain explicit stakeholder validation (CAA program manager sign-off, MAA liaison confirmation) for each of F19-F22 before they enter the SRS. For REQ017 (Public Engagement): CAP 1616 mandates consultation, but the workbench's scope (internal regulator tool) may exclude public-facing features—clarify if the CAA will use this tool to manage engagement, or if engagement happens outside. For REQ018 (Classified Evidence): JSP 440 / CAP 722H handling is critical for MOD partnerships, but the Vibe finding 'Status below tolerable' (2 stakeholder groups vs 5 required) signals under-engagement. For REQ019 (Environmental): Noise/species disturbance is operationally real but not mentioned in original user stories—escalate to CAA licensing inspector or environmental compliance function for ownership.

findings.finding_title: Five foundational terms lack authoritative definition; stakeholder alignment unconfirmed
findings.requirement: REQ002 (swarm-aware SORA evidence, defensibly), REQ005 (containment), REQ010 (meaningful human control), REQ011 (independent termination)
findings.source_reference: DeFOSPAM glossary: 50 of 88 terms unverified; DeFOSPAM undefined_term findings (16 total); Vibe QC finding #3 (MeaningfulHumanControl gap)
findings.reasoning: Dorothy identifies 16 undefined terms critical to requirements interpretation. Five are regulatory anchors: (1) 'swarm-aware' (REQ002)—without this, SORA assessors have no criteria for what constitutes swarm-specific risk evidence vs. single-aircraft evidence; (2) 'defensibly' (REQ002)—evocative but undefined; regulators and applicants may interpret 'defensible' differently (e.g., statistical 99% confidence vs. expert judgment). (3) 'meaningful human control' (REQ010)—contested across CAA (emphasis on workload, fatigue, cognitive load) vs. MAA (emphasis on override capability, takeover time) vs. applicant (emphasis on UI clarity). (4) 'containment' (REQ005)—does not specify whether this is procedural, technical, or both, or what happens if geofence fails (Vibe defect: 'geofence system failure—no specification of what happens'). (5) 'independent termination' (REQ011)—ambiguous: independent of what? Control link? Crew? Software architecture? These gaps allow DeFOSPAM finding 'unpredictable_outcome' (14 instances) where the software cannot know the true intent.
findings.recommendation: Before SRS approval, conduct joint CAA/MAA/JARUS working group session to define these five terms. Publish glossary with examples from prior BVLOS swarm trials (e.g., JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm case studies, UK BVLOS trials at Porthcawl/Cardington). For 'meaningful human control': adopt ISO 9646 workload scale + NASA-TLX measurement protocol, with explicit thresholds (e.g., workload <= 60 on NASA-TLX during nominal, crew takeover within 2 seconds). For 'containment': explicitly separate procedural (e.g., TDA geometry) from technical (geofence + RTL) + contingency (manual takeover), and define failure sequence (geofence fails -> RTL triggered -> crew manual override). Require applicants to use these definitions in their evidence packages.

findings.finding_title: Geofence breach, link loss, and DAA resolution outcomes undefined—SRS silent on safety actions
findings.requirement: REQ005 (Geofence/Containment Enforcement), REQ006 (C2 Link Assurance), REQ007 (DAA Performance), REQ008 (Swarm Decision-Making)
findings.source_reference: DeFOSPAM missing_outcome findings (14 total): REQ005 geofence breach outcome unspecified; REQ006 link loss outcome undefined; REQ007 evasion strategy unspecified; REQ008 fallback action missing. Vibe QC finding #5 (DAAPerformance: 88% success vs 99.5% tolerable).
findings.reasoning: The SRS states what outcomes are needed ('containment so a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS', 'link loss is survivable', 'encounters are resolved predictably') but does not specify the MECHANISM or DECISION TREE. REQ005 says the system shall provide 'automatic containment' but does not say: What triggers containment? (Geofence breach detected? Timeout? Crew command?) What is the fail-safe? (RTL to home? Parachute? Hover and await recovery?) What if that fails? (E.g., RTL fails due to GPS denial—then what?) REQ006 says link loss must be 'survivable' but omits: Survivable for how long? (30 seconds? 5 minutes?) With what autonomy? (Autonomous hover? Return-to-land? Swarm holds formation?) What triggers crew takeover? REQ007 (DAA) says encounters 'shall be resolved predictably' but the Vibe QC finding shows current performance is 88%, regulatory minimum is 99.5%—a 11.5% gap. The SRS does not specify: Do cooperative encounters trigger different maneuvers than non-cooperative? Does swarm coordinate de-confliction, or does each aircraft independently avoid? What is the priority if two conflicting avoidance commands emerge? These are not implementation details; they are behavioral requirements the regulator must assure before flight.
findings.recommendation: Decompose REQ005-008 into explicit outcome rules: REQ005a: 'IF geofence boundary breach detected THEN RTL initiated within 2 seconds'; REQ005b: 'IF RTL fails THEN crew receives 60dB audio alert + swarm transitions to autonomous hover at last-valid altitude, maintaining separation from nearest aircraft >= min-separation'; REQ005c: 'IF hover fails THEN [emergency parachute deployment / immediate descent procedure per applicant design]'. Similar specificity for REQ006 (link loss) and REQ007 (DAA). Require applicants to provide decision trees and state machine diagrams in ConOps, with explicit handling of cross-cutting failures (e.g., simultaneous geofence breach + link loss + crew incapacitation). Use Vibe target setting to establish measurable success criteria (e.g., 'DAA performance >= 99.5% against DO-365 scenario library').

findings.finding_title: Vague qualifier 'complete' used in two contexts with conflicting meanings
findings.original_text: REQ001: 'complete Operational Authorisation pack' vs REQ012: 'complete recorded evidence'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 identified that 'complete' has two distinct and conflicting meanings: (1) REQ001 context: artefact presence and substantive content (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, etc.). (2) REQ012 context: evidentiary completeness (all telemetry, video, audio logs present). These cannot share a single acceptance criterion. A pack with all required documents may lack forensic completeness; conversely, complete telemetry logs may derive from an incomplete application package.
findings.proposed_rewrite: REQ001: 'Pack Complete' = all 8 mandatory artefacts present with substantive content >= 90% section coverage. REQ012: 'Evidence Complete' = all 6 telemetry categories (state, decisions, link quality, crew actions, video, audio) present at specified sampling rates with < 100 ms gaps.
findings.reasoning: This is the most pervasive semantic ambiguity across the SRS. Using 'complete' for both artefact intake and telemetry logging creates risk that case officers and investigators will apply inconsistent acceptance criteria. Dorothy-8 rated confidence 10/10 because the distinction has material regulatory impact: incomplete application intake should halt a case (return-for-clarification), whereas incomplete telemetry might be acceptable if incident was minor. These are opposite decisions.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Replace 'complete' with two distinct terms: 'Pack Completeness' (artefact presence) and 'Evidentiary Completeness' (telemetry presence). Update SRS glossary with explicit distinction. Train case officers on the two acceptance criteria.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'swarm-aware' is foundational to SORA assessment without clear criteria
findings.original_text: REQ002: 'As a CAA SORA assessor I want swarm-aware SORA evidence so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly.'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-1 and Chris's language file both identify 'swarm-aware' as critical undefined term (confidence 10/10). The term appears in REQ002 (SORA assessment) and REQ008 (swarm decision-making review) but lacks clear definition. Does 'swarm-aware SORA evidence' mean: (1) evidence addressing only swarm-specific risks (coordination, emergent behavior)? (2) evidence addressing both single-aircraft AND swarm-specific risks? (3) evidence using a specialized SORA methodology for swarms? Without clarity, SORA assessors lack criteria for what evidence to demand. This impacts SAIL and OSO setpoints, which directly govern trial scope.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Swarm-aware SORA evidence shall explicitly address: (1) collective behavior arising from inter-vehicle coordination (formation adherence, swarm cohesion); (2) coordination dependencies (if one aircraft fails, cascading risks to others); (3) emergent risks not present in single-aircraft SORA (separation loss during coordinated evasion, deadlock in distributed decision-making, Byzantine aircraft in peer-voting architecture). Evidence shall reference hazard analysis (FTA, STAMP, or HARA) that identifies swarm-specific hazard modes, not just individual aircraft failure modes.
findings.reasoning: This term is foundational to REQ002, which is a gate requirement for SORA assessment. Without defining 'swarm-aware,' SORA assessors (different individuals across trials) will develop inconsistent interpretations. Some may accept single-aircraft SORA with swarm annotations; others may demand swarm-specific methodology (JARUS SORA Swarm Extension). This inconsistency violates IEEE 830 principle of unambiguous requirements.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Add 'swarm-aware' to SRS glossary with explicit definition (see proposed rewrite). Reference JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm guidance and enumerate 3 required evidence components (collective behavior, coordination dependencies, emergent risks). Update REQ002 to reference this definition.

findings.finding_title: Vague qualifier 'defensibly' lacks measurable criteria for SORA approval
findings.original_text: REQ002: 'so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-3 identified 'defensibly' as undefined vague success criterion (confidence 10/10). What does it mean to set SAIL and OSO 'defensibly'? Does it require: (1) defensibility against peer review? (2) defensibility under litigation/investigation? (3) defensibility per JARUS methodology? (4) defensibility per historical CAA precedent? Without explicit criteria, SORA assessors lack objective gate for approval. Each assessor may apply different 'defensibility standard,' leading to inconsistent SAIL/OSO setpoints across trials.
findings.proposed_rewrite: SAIL and OSO requirements shall be set defensibly when: (1) justified through documented hazard analysis (FTA, STAMP, HARA) with explicit link from each OSO to identified hazard mode; (2) aligned with JARUS SORA v2.5 methodology and CAA precedent (reference 2+ prior authorizations using same SAIL level as evidence of defensibility); (3) reviewable by independent safety expert who can assess sufficiency of risk controls relative to identified hazards; (4) documented in Structured Argument (GSN, CAF, or similar) showing logical chain from hazards → requirements → verification.
findings.reasoning: SAIL and OSO are regulatory gates that permit or deny trial progression. 'Defensibly' is the success criterion for these gates, yet it is subjective. Different assessors may have different thresholds for what constitutes defensible evidence. This violates IEEE 830 unambiguity: every requirement must have one interpretation.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Define 'defensibly' operationally in SRS glossary. Reference JARUS methodology, require GSN or CAF structured argument, and require documentation of CAA precedent. Update REQ002 to reference this definition.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'worst-credible behaviour' lacks credibility boundary
findings.original_text: REQ003: 'I want a clear ConOps and ODD so that I can judge nominal and worst-credible behaviour'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-5 identified 'worst-credible behaviour' as requiring workshop to define credibility boundary (confidence 10/10). Chris's language file defines it as 'Most severe adverse system performance or failure mode reasonably foreseeable.' But 'reasonably foreseeable' is subjective. Does it include: (1) only single failures (e.g., one engine loss)? (2) common-mode failures (e.g., simultaneous GPS loss on all aircraft)? (3) software defects (e.g., swarm decision-making logic error in all aircraft)? (4) human errors (e.g., crew simultaneous incapacitation)? (5) environmental extremes (e.g., 100 mph winds)? Without defining the credibility boundary, safety-case reviewers will apply inconsistent thresholds. Some may demand controls for software defects; others may dismiss them as not 'credible.' This creates regulatory inconsistency.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Worst-credible behaviour shall be defined as single-failure scenarios that affect one or more aircraft but do NOT assume: (1) simultaneous failure of functionally independent systems; (2) common-mode failures affecting all aircraft identically due to software defect; (3) human errors affecting multiple crew simultaneously (single incapacitation is credible; simultaneous incapacitation is not). Examples of worst-credible: GPS loss on one aircraft, C2 link loss, geofence breach, engine failure, battery depletion. Examples NOT credible: simultaneous GPS loss on all aircraft (common-mode), entire swarm decision-making algorithm failure (software defect), both pilots incapacitated simultaneously (human error assumption). Safety case shall address all worst-credible scenarios through containment or crew intervention.
findings.reasoning: 'Worst-credible behaviour' is the upper bound for what safety case must address. Setting this boundary too low (includes common-mode failures) may make trial unachievable; setting too high (excludes common-mode failures) may miss critical risks. The boundary directly impacts trial feasibility and safety assurance. Dorothy-5 rated this confidence 10/10 because it is a foundational assumption for safety case completeness.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Define credibility boundary explicitly in SRS glossary. Specify single-failure assumption (not common-mode, not software defects, not multi-human errors). Enumerate examples of credible vs. not-credible scenarios. Update REQ003 to reference this definition.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'containment' lacks design specification and verification approach
findings.original_text: REQ005: 'I want automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-4 identified 'containment' as undefined with 5 critical subcriteria unknown: (1) boundary tolerance (how far beyond geofence before containment triggers?), (2) fail-safe mechanism (what triggers containment if primary system fails?), (3) design standard (DO-178C, MIL-STD-882, IEC 61508?), (4) environmental factors (wind, GPS multipath), (5) verification approach (simulation, flight test, both?). Without these specifications, containment design may be inadequate. A 1-meter boundary tolerance is vastly different from 100-meter tolerance. A software-only containment is vastly different from hardware kill-switch. These create an order-of-magnitude difference in safety assurance.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Containment shall be designed per DO-178C (Software Considerations in Airborne Systems and Equipment Certification) as a safety-critical function. System shall include: (1) geofence monitor (onboard GPS polling at 10 Hz minimum, boundary tolerance ±10 meters); (2) primary containment action (Return-to-Launch if GPS available, failover to climb-and-hold if not); (3) fail-safe trigger (upon geofence breach detection, containment shall engage within 500 milliseconds independent of ground crew command); (4) environmental robustness (geofence monitor shall account for ±5-meter GPS accuracy variation, shall not false-trigger in urban multipath environments). Verification shall combine: (1) software testing (unit tests, integration tests, HITL simulation), (2) flight test (boundary stress test with artificial geofence breaches), (3) CAA witness and approval. Failure of containment shall result in mandatory trial suspension and investigation.
findings.reasoning: Containment is a critical safety function preventing fly-aways and loss of control. Undefined containment design creates risk that implementers will choose inadequate boundaries or fail-safe mechanisms. Dorothy-4 rated confidence 10/10 because the term is both high-risk and completely unspecified.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Define 'containment' in SRS glossary with all 5 subcriteria (boundary tolerance, fail-safe mechanism, design standard, environmental factors, verification approach). Update REQ005 to reference this definition and specify DO-178C compliance, 500-ms response time, and verification through flight test.

findings.finding_title: Undefined outcome for geofence breach (CRITICAL SAFETY)
findings.original_text: REQ005: 'I want automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: REQ005 specifies the desired outcome (prevent fly-away) but does NOT specify what actions the system takes when geofence breach is detected. Does containment mean: (1) immediately terminate flight? (2) trigger return-to-launch? (3) climb to safe altitude and hold? (4) alert crew and wait for response? Without specifying the action, implementers will make different choices. Some may design for immediate termination; others may design for RTL, leading to different failure modes and safety profiles.
findings.proposed_rewrite: REQ005 outcome: Upon geofence breach detection (aircraft position exceeds boundary by > 10 meters for > 2 seconds), the system shall automatically execute primary containment action: (1) if GPS is available (confidence > 95%), execute Return-to-Launch to final landing location; (2) if GPS is unavailable or unreliable, execute failover: climb to 500 feet AGL minimum altitude and hold level flight. Simultaneously, crew shall be alerted via audio/visual notification within 1 second of breach detection. If crew intervention is required (e.g., to override RTL due to obstacle), crew shall manually select containment action via control switch within 10 seconds. Failure to engage containment within 500 milliseconds shall trigger emergency termination (if equipped).
findings.reasoning: This is a critical safety requirement. Geofence breach is a failure mode that could lead to loss of separation from other traffic or impact on ground. The system's response to this failure is safety-critical. DeFOSPAM Observation-2 identified this as missing outcome with CRITICAL SAFETY tag. Without specifying the action, trial safety depends on implementer judgment rather than regulatory requirement.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Add explicit outcome to REQ005 specifying: (1) primary action (RTL if GPS available), (2) failover (climb-and-hold if GPS lost), (3) response time (500 ms), (4) crew alert timing (1 s). This is a gate requirement for trial authorization; geofence breach response must be demonstrable in pre-flight testing.

findings.finding_title: Vague phrase 'link loss survivable' lacks time thresholds and failure mode boundaries
findings.original_text: REQ006: 'I want authorised monitored C2 and inter-vehicle links so that link loss is survivable and non-interfering'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Flo-1 identified 'link loss survivable' as ambiguous on time, failure mode, and crew role (confidence 10/10). What does 'survivable' mean? Does it require: (1) survival for 5 seconds (minimal)? (2) survival for 1 minute? (3) survival until RTL completes? Does it apply to: (1) temporary link loss (signal drops for < 1 second)? (2) sustained link loss (> 1 minute)? (3) permanent link loss? What crew actions are required to 'survive' link loss: (1) passive monitoring? (2) active takeover? (3) activation of failover modes? Without clear boundaries, different implementers will design for different survival times, leading to inconsistent operational procedures and safety profiles.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Link loss survivable means: (1) upon C2 link loss (signal quality below minimum threshold for > 1 second), aircraft shall automatically execute failover behavior (RTL if GPS available, climb-and-hold if GPS lost) without requiring crew intervention; (2) aircraft shall maintain containment (separation from other aircraft and geofence adherence) for minimum 5 minutes during C2 loss, sufficient for crew to re-establish link or initiate termination; (3) inter-vehicle links shall provide swarm cohesion during C2 loss (e.g., formation hold, coordinated RTL) sufficient to prevent uncontrolled separation or collision. Survival is verified through: (1) simulation (C2 loss scenario), (2) HITL testing (crew observes failover), (3) flight test (actual C2 loss triggered and monitored).
findings.reasoning: C2 link loss is a credible failure mode for BVLOS operations. The system's response to this failure is safety-critical. 'Survivable' without specifying time or action criteria is too vague. Different operators may interpret 'survivable' as 10 seconds, 1 minute, or 5 minutes—vastly different operational windows. Flo-1 rated confidence 10/10 because link loss is a known failure mode with real consequences.
findings.recommendation: MAJOR: Define 'link loss survivable' operationally in SRS glossary. Specify: (1) link loss threshold (signal quality < X dB for > 1 second), (2) failover action (RTL or climb-and-hold), (3) survival time (minimum 5 minutes), (4) separation maintenance (prevent collision). Update REQ006 to reference this definition.

findings.finding_title: Undefined phrase 'resolved predictably' lacks algorithm specification and determinism criteria
findings.original_text: REQ007: 'I want DO-365 / F3442-aligned evidence for each aircraft and coordinated swarm de-confliction so that cooperative and non-cooperative encounters are resolved predictably'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Paul-1 identified 'resolved predictably' as undefined on mechanism and verification (confidence 10/10). 'Predictable' could mean: (1) deterministic (same input always produces same output)? (2) foreseeable (crew can anticipate the outcome)? (3) validated (verified through test to produce consistent behavior)? Without specifying the algorithm and determinism criteria, different implementers may design probabilistic de-confliction (ML-based) vs. deterministic de-confliction (rule-based). These produce vastly different reliability and assurance profiles.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Encounter resolution is predictable when: (1) for any given encounter geometry (relative bearing, range, closing rate), the de-confliction algorithm always issues the same recommended maneuver (deterministic, not probabilistic); (2) all aircraft in swarm follow the preferred maneuver within 2 seconds of issuance; (3) resulting maneuver is foreseeable to all participants (clearly documented in operational procedures). De-confliction algorithm shall be: (1) rule-based (explicit decision logic, e.g., 'If closing rate > X and range < Y, climb 500 feet'), (2) peer-reviewed (independent safety expert validates decision tree), (3) verified through: Monte Carlo simulation (1000+ encounter scenarios), HITL testing (crew observes recommended maneuvers), and flight test (actual encounters with safety backup). Probabilistic algorithms (ML, fuzzy logic) are NOT acceptable because they cannot guarantee deterministic outcome for the same encounter.
findings.reasoning: Detect-and-avoid and de-confliction are safety-critical functions. Predictable behavior is essential for crew to trust the system and to validate safety cases. Paul-1 rated confidence 10/10 because 'predictably' directly impacts regulatory assurance—regulators need to be able to forecast what the swarm will do in any encounter scenario.
findings.recommendation: MAJOR: Define 'resolved predictably' operationally. Specify: (1) deterministic algorithm (rule-based, not probabilistic), (2) decision tree documentation, (3) execution latency (2-second maneuver following), (4) verification through simulation and flight test. Exclude probabilistic algorithms from acceptable de-confliction methods.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'forensically explainable' sets unrealistic standard for ML post-hoc explainability
findings.original_text: REQ009: 'I want an AMLAS / SACE case a frozen-model manifest and explainability evidence so that ML components are assurable and forensically explainable'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-6 identified 'forensically explainable' as undefined and unrealistic for deep learning (confidence 10/10). Perfect post-hoc explainability for neural networks is mathematically infeasible—no algorithm can perfectly reconstruct why a 100-layer deep network made a specific decision. But REQ009 demands 'forensically explainable' without defining achievable standard. Applicants may interpret this as requiring impossible perfect explainability, leading to trial rejection on infeasible criteria.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Forensically explainable means: sufficient post-hoc analysis capability to identify which input features contributed to a decision (via SHAP, LIME, or attention maps), sufficient to determine if model was operating within training data distribution, sufficient to link decision to model version per Frozen-Model Manifest. Perfect reconstruction of neural network decisions is not required. For small models (decision trees, rule-based systems), explainability shall approach perfect. For deep learning, explainability shall be sufficient for accident investigation, specifically: (1) identify anomalous inputs that triggered unexpected decisions, (2) identify if decision was within learned decision boundary, (3) identify if incident involved model behavior trained to handle scenario or out-of-distribution behavior. Explainability methods shall be peer-reviewed by ML safety expert.
findings.reasoning: Deep learning explainability is a known hard problem in AI safety. Demanding perfect 'forensic explainability' is setting an impossible bar. Dorothy-6 rated confidence 10/10 because this term, if left undefined, will cause trial rejections on infeasible criteria. Applicants need clear, achievable standards for ML assurance.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Redefine 'forensically explainable' in SRS glossary to realistic standard: sufficient for investigation, not perfect reconstruction. Enumerate specific techniques (SHAP, LIME, mechanistic interpretability for small models). Specify that perfect explainability is not required for deep learning. Update REQ009 to reference this definition and exclude impossible standards.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'frozen-model manifest' vague on retention, reconstruction, and security
findings.original_text: REQ009: 'I want an AMLAS / SACE case a frozen-model manifest and explainability evidence'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-7 identified 'frozen-model manifest' as undefined with 7 subcriteria unknown: (1) data retention (how long must training data be preserved?), (2) duration (how long is model frozen?), (3) security (how is proprietary model protected?), (4) manifest format (XML, JSON, proprietary?), (5) proprietary handling (how to handle vendor IP?), (6) ownership (who owns frozen-model documentation?), (7) reconstruction timing (when can model be reconstructed for investigation?). Without these specifications, applicants and investigators will have inconsistent expectations about model freezing and evidence preservation.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Frozen-Model Manifest shall document for each ML model deployed: (1) model version ID and release date; (2) training dataset (hash value SHA256 for reproducibility, not full dataset retention); (3) hyperparameters (learning rate, regularization, architecture details); (4) performance metrics (validation accuracy, F1 score for safety-critical outputs); (5) operational constraints (input domain, confidence thresholds); (6) certification that model weights and architecture shall remain immutable throughout trial duration (minimum 2 years). Manifest shall be stored securely (encrypted at rest, access logged) with copies held by CAA and applicant. Proprietary vendor details (training optimization, internal algorithms) may be redacted; manifest must include sufficient detail for regulator to assess model scope and performance. Upon incident investigation, manifest enables reconstruction of model behavior and validation against expected performance. Manifest is considered part of trial evidence and shall be retained per REQ012 (minimum 2 years or until investigation closes).
findings.reasoning: ML model freezing is critical for forensic reproducibility. Without specifying what must be documented (version, training data hash, hyperparameters, performance, constraints), different applicants will freeze different aspects. Some may freeze only model weights; others may also freeze training data metadata. This creates inconsistency in trial assurance.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Define 'frozen-model manifest' in SRS glossary with all 7 subcriteria: (1) format (SHA256 hash, hyperparameters, metrics), (2) storage (encrypted, access-logged, dual custody CAA + applicant), (3) duration (2 years minimum), (4) proprietary protection (redactions permitted with sufficient detail preserved), (5) reconstruction timing (on-demand for investigation). Update REQ009 to reference this definition and specify mandatory Manifest fields.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'meaningful human control' lacks measurable engagement criteria
findings.original_text: REQ010: 'I want workload duty and take-over evidence so that meaningful human control is demonstrated throughout'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-2 identified 'meaningful human control' as critical undefined term (confidence 10/10). Chris's language file defines it as 'Active, informed crew awareness and retained authority to perceive system state, understand autonomous actions, and intervene before safety-critical decisions.' But no measurable criteria are provided. Does 'meaningful' require: (1) crew maintains situational awareness (how frequently? every second? every minute?)? (2) crew retains authority (can override any decision? only some decisions?)? (3) crew can intervene in time (5-second window? 10-second window?). Without measurable criteria, different trials will be authorized with vastly different levels of human control, creating inconsistency in regulatory assurance.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Meaningful human control is demonstrated when: (1) crew maintains continuous situational awareness: crew receives system state updates (position, airspeed, altitude, decisions) minimum 1 per second; (2) crew retains authority: crew can override autonomous decisions and issue manual commands at all times with override latency < 2 seconds; (3) crew has intervention capability: crew can perceive off-nominal events and intervene before safety-critical outcomes, with time window >= 5 seconds for evasion maneuvers, >= 10 seconds for return-to-launch. These measures shall be verified through: (1) workload assessment (NASA-TLX or equivalent, target < 75% cognitive capacity nominal, < 85% contingency), (2) simulator testing (10+ takeover scenarios, crew success rate >= 95%), (3) flight test observer validation (crew demonstrates awareness and intervention capability).
findings.reasoning: Human control of autonomous systems is a regulatory requirement. 'Meaningful' is subjective without measurable criteria. Different trials may interpret 'meaningful' as requiring different levels of crew engagement. Dorothy-2 rated confidence 10/10 because this is a foundational regulatory principle that must be unambiguous across all trials.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Define 'meaningful human control' in SRS glossary with specific, measurable criteria: (1) situational awareness update frequency (1 Hz minimum), (2) override latency (< 2 seconds), (3) intervention time windows (5-10 seconds), (4) workload limits (75-85% NASA-TLX), (5) simulator success rate (>= 95%). Update REQ010 to reference this definition and require pre-trial demonstration in HITL simulator.

findings.finding_title: Ambiguous use of 'complete recorded evidence' conflicts with 'complete Operational Authorisation pack'
findings.original_text: REQ012: 'I want complete recorded evidence accepted MOR obligations and a DPIA'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: REQ012 uses 'complete recorded evidence' to mean telemetry and logs (what is recorded during flight). REQ001 uses 'complete Operational Authorisation pack' to mean artefacts (what is submitted before flight). These are two completely different definitions of 'complete' with opposite meanings: REQ001 'complete' means absence of gaps; REQ012 'complete' means comprehensive coverage of all telemetry types. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 rated confidence 10/10 because this is the most pervasive ambiguity causing confusion between application intake and incident investigation phases.
findings.proposed_rewrite: REQ001: 'complete Operational Authorisation Pack' = 8 mandatory artefacts present with substantive content >= 90% section coverage. REQ012: 'complete recorded evidence' = 6 telemetry categories (aircraft state, autonomy decisions, link quality, crew actions, video, audio) sampled at specified rates with < 100 ms gaps throughout flight.
findings.reasoning: This is the single most pervasive ambiguity across all 20 REQs. Using 'complete' for both artefact intake (REQ001) and telemetry logging (REQ012) creates semantic collision. The two definitions are incompatible and require different acceptance criteria, measurement methods, and stakeholders. Dorothy-8 identified this as CRITICAL because it impacts two separate regulatory gates with opposite consequences.
findings.recommendation: CRITICAL: Rename REQ001 'complete' to 'Pack Completeness' and REQ012 'complete' to 'Evidence Completeness' to disambiguate. Define each separately in glossary with explicit acceptance criteria, measurement methods, and responsible stakeholders.

findings.finding_title: 20 TBDs span critical swarm-safety methodology gaps blocking REQ002 (swarm-aware SORA assessment)
findings.gap_description: Odin REQ002 rewrite operationalizes swarm-aware SORA evidence (3 components: inter-vehicle coordination, emergent risks, documented hazard analysis) but JARUS SORA v2.5 methodology is single-aircraft-focused. No standardized taxonomy exists for swarm interdependency failures (loss-of-separation cascades, deadlock, single-point-of-failure propagation). Trial applicants cannot credibly assess swarm-specific SORA without this methodology.

tbd_details.reason: Civil aviation SORA standard does not yet cover multi-agent interdependency failures.
tbd_details.responsible: CAA + JARUS Joint Working Group (SORA domain experts); applicant safety engineers
tbd_details.blocking_path: First trial cohort can proceed with applicant-proposed swarm-aware evidence (no standard yet); WS-001 workshop must produce SORA 2.0 Swarm Supplement to operationalize REQ002 for future trials.

findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-1 identified 'swarm-aware' as critical undefined term (confidence 10/10). Chris language file defines swarm-aware but provides no methodology. Odin rewrite references JARUS v2.5 but does not address swarm-specific gaps. Grace goal GOAL-002 (Assure Swarm-Aware Safety Assessment) is the highest-risk goal for first trial cohort because methodology does not yet exist.
findings.recommendation: Establish WS-001 swarm SORA methodology workshop (Q2 2026): CAA + JARUS liaison + industry safety engineers. Define: (1) hazard taxonomy specific to swarm (loss-of-separation modes, separation-loss cascade triggers); (2) analysis methodology (FTA extensions for multi-agent systems, STAMP for distributed control); (3) acceptance criteria (e.g., 'quantified maximum cascade depth = 2 aircraft, probability of cascade < 0.1% per flight'); (4) evidence template (documentation structure for applicant submission). Publish SORA 2.0 Swarm Supplement by end Q3 2026.

findings.finding_title: Containment design standard undefined (5 critical subcriteria per Dorothy-4)
findings.gap_description: REQ005 (Geofence/Containment Enforcement) specifies 10-meter boundary tolerance and 500-millisecond response time but provides no design justification or standard. Five subcriteria undefined: (1) fail-safe mechanism rationale; (2) environmental stress factors (wind gust, sensor degradation); (3) design standard reference (DO-178C? IEC 61508?); (4) verification approach (software testing alone vs. hardware-in-loop); (5) single-vehicle vs. swarm-specific design differences.

tbd_details.reason: No industry standard yet governs swarm containment design; 10m and 500ms are heuristic, not evidence-based.
tbd_details.responsible: CAA autonomy reviewer + design-standard consortium (autonomy engineers, safety engineers, test engineers)
tbd_details.blocking_path: First trial must negotiate containment acceptance criteria with CAA autonomy reviewer per-trial; WS-002 workshop must establish baseline design standard for future trials.

findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-4 identified 'containment' as critical undefined term with 5 subcriteria. Odin rewrite specifies mechanism and response time but not design rationale. Containment is safety-critical per Grace goal GOAL-003 (Ensure Containment & Flight Safety) but lacks formal design basis. Risk: applicant may propose inadequate containment; CAA reviewer lacks standard to assess adequacy.
findings.recommendation: Establish WS-002 containment design-standard workshop (Q2 2026): CAA + autonomy engineers + safety engineers. Develop: (1) containment failure-mode taxonomy (geofence-sensor loss, computation latency, motor failure, firmware bug); (2) failure-rate assumptions (design basis accident: geofence breach must not exceed 0.1% per flight hour); (3) fail-safe mechanism specification (mechanical kill-switch + software enforcement + procedural backup); (4) design reference standards (adapt DO-178C for autonomy, adapt IEC 61508 for safety-critical system); (5) environmental stress-testing plan (wind gust, vibration, thermal, sensor noise injection). Publish CAA Containment Design Standard by end Q2 2026.

findings.finding_title: Meaningful human control measurable criteria undefined (Dorothy-2, international debate ongoing)
findings.gap_description: REQ010 (Human Oversight and Crew Fitness) demands 'meaningful human control' but this term lacks measurable criteria. Odin rewrite operationalizes through 3 proxies (situational awareness 1 Hz, override latency <2 seconds, intervention time window 5-10 seconds) and workload thresholds (75% nominal / 85% contingency), but no consensus exists on whether these thresholds satisfy 'meaningful' control for autonomous swarm systems. International debate ongoing between Civil Society (advocates stricter human authority) and Military precedent (accepts more autonomy with less human intervention).

tbd_details.reason: No accepted international standard for 'meaningful human control' in autonomous systems; civil-aviation authority has not yet defined measurable criteria specific to BVLOS swarm.
tbd_details.responsible: CAA Human Factors Group + MAA (military authority on human control precedent); university human-factors research partner
tbd_details.blocking_path: First trial (Phase 1 VLOS) is not blocked by MHC definition because VLOS is fully human-controlled. Phase 2+ trials cannot proceed without CAA-approved MHC criteria.

findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-2 identified 'meaningful human control' as critical undefined term (confidence 10/10). Chris language defines it as 'Active, informed crew awareness and retained authority.' Odin operationalizes with measurable thresholds (1 Hz, <2s, workload %). But empirical basis for these thresholds lacking. Grace goal GOAL-004 (Assure Autonomous Decision-Making & Human Control) depends on MHC definition for acceptability criteria.
findings.recommendation: Establish WS-003 human factors workshop (Q3 2026): CAA + university human-factors research lab + applicant crew trainers. Conduct: (1) simulator study (swarm scenario playback with human subjects) measuring take-over response time, workload under various scenario complexities, crew situation awareness recovery time; (2) evidence synthesis (compare proposed thresholds to manned-aviation precedent, military UAV precedent); (3) develop 'Meaningful Human Control Assurance Guidance' specifying: situation-awareness update frequency (operationalize '1 Hz'), override-latency acceptance (justify '<2s'), workload thresholds (validate '75%/85%'). Publish guidance and update REQ010 by end Q3 2026.

findings.finding_title: REQ008 (Emergent Behaviour Assurance) vs REQ010 (Meaningful Human Control) - Logical Conflict
findings.conflict_description: REQ008 requires assurance of autonomous emergent behaviour in swarm decision-making (decisions not explicitly commanded). REQ010 requires meaningful human control throughout operations. These are mutually incompatible: if emergent behaviour is autonomous and beyond human command, how can human control be maintained 'throughout'? The requirements create a genuine contradiction between operator automation expectations and regulator control expectations.
findings.reasoning: REQ008 user story: 'decision-making architecture airborne limits and emergent-behaviour evidence so that I can assure what the swarm will do collectively.' This implies swarm makes decisions autonomously at swarm level (emergent). REQ010 user story: 'meaningful human control is demonstrated throughout.' If swarm makes emergent decisions autonomously, the crew cannot intervene 'throughout' without halting emergent behaviour. This is the classic autonomy-vs-control conflict: Alan's stakeholder analysis (openperformance-output) flags this as irreconcilable without explicit decision authority boundaries. Two REQs cannot both be satisfied without defining which decisions are autonomous (emergent) and which require human approval.
findings.recommendation: Define explicit decision authority partitioning: (1) Classify swarm decisions into two categories: SAFETY_CRITICAL (geofence breach, link loss response, DAA avoidance) and OPERATIONAL (formation adjustment, route optimization); (2) REQ008 applies to OPERATIONAL emergent behaviour only; crew assures bounds via ConOps ODD; (3) REQ010 applies to SAFETY_CRITICAL decisions; crew retains decision authority override for any safety-critical action; (4) add requirement: 'Crew shall be able to override any safety-critical decision within 2-second window before execution. System shall display decision rationale and provide explicit APPROVE/REJECT buttons for safety-critical actions.' (5) Define which decisions fall into each category in ConOps; use phased gates (REQ016) to test decision authority boundaries.

findings.finding_title: REQ005 (Automatic Containment) vs REQ011 (Independent Termination) - Logical Conflict on Mechanism
findings.conflict_description: REQ005 requires automatic geofence containment (aircraft cannot leave boundary). REQ011 requires independent termination (motors cut if containment fails). These are conflicting outcomes: if containment is absolute (REQ005), why is termination ever needed (REQ011)? Conversely, if termination is needed (REQ011), containment must be fallible (contradicting REQ005). The requirements do not specify the sequencing or fallback chain.
findings.reasoning: REQ005 user story: 'automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS.' The word 'automatic' and 'cannot' imply absolute prevention—containment always works. REQ011 user story: 'independent termination a contingency matrix ... so that the trial can be made safe in any off-nominal state.' This implies containment CAN fail (otherwise why terminate?). Paul's scenario findings (P001, P002) flag this: 'containment enforcement vs independent termination overlap.' If REQ005's containment always succeeds, REQ011's termination is redundant. If REQ011 assumes containment fails, REQ005 is false. The glossary notes this tension: 'containment' (REQ005) is a preventive mechanism; 'flight termination' (REQ011) is a reaction. They are sequential (prevention first, termination as fallback), but REQs do not say that.
findings.recommendation: Clarify the fallback hierarchy: (1) REQ005 requires PRIMARY CONTAINMENT: geofence logic onboard aircraft prevents boundary crossing via motor soft-stop and crew alert (preventive). (2) REQ011 requires SECONDARY TERMINATION: if primary containment fails (geofence hardware failure, GPS loss), independent termination (motor cutoff) activates as last resort. (3) Add explicit requirement: 'Containment shall follow fail-safe fallback: (a) On geofence approach (within 50m), crew alert + reduced autonomy; (b) on geofence breach (aircraft crosses boundary), immediate motor soft-stop to hover thrust, crew alert; (c) if soft-stop fails to contain aircraft within 5 seconds, independent termination system activates (motors cut, controlled descent). (4) Test strategy: verify PRIMARY containment prevents 99.9% of breaches; verify SECONDARY termination handles 1-in-1000 containment failures. Define specific thresholds and test scenarios.

findings.finding_title: REQ017 (Public Engagement) vs REQ018 (Classified Evidence) - Logical Conflict on Disclosure
findings.conflict_description: REQ017 (absent, but implied by CAP 1616) requires public stakeholder engagement and notification. REQ018 (absent, but implied by CAP 722H/JSP 440) requires classified MOD evidence to be handled under security protocols. These are mutually exclusive: public engagement requires transparency; classified handling requires secrecy. The requirements do not specify how evidence can be both transparent (REQ017) and classified (REQ018) simultaneously.
findings.reasoning: REQ017 notes: 'No user story covers community consultation press/media handling or prior-notification to landowners. Strongly implied by CAP 1616 and GDPR but absent.' REQ018 notes: 'No user story addresses how classified MOD evidence is presented to the CAA under CAP 722H / JSP 440 without compromise to either side.' These are both ABSENT user stories, yet they represent genuine regulatory obligations. If a trial involves MOD-developed autonomy (classified), and requires public notification (CAP 1616), the system must support two contradictory evidence workflows: (1) PUBLIC version of trial plan (redacted, unclassified); (2) CLASSIFIED version for MOD/CAA review. But REQs do not address this segregation. Flo's findings flag this: 'Classification-Aware Evidence Segregation & Access Control' is a hidden feature never explicitly required.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit user stories for REQ017 and REQ018 with segregated evidence handling: (1) REQ017 (PUBLIC ENGAGEMENT): 'As a CAA community liaison I want to publish a redacted trial summary and conduct stakeholder consultation so that CAP 1616 obligations are met and public concerns are documented. Evidence: public-facing trial description, stakeholder meeting minutes, pre-notification to landowners, media release (if applicable). (2) REQ018 (CLASSIFIED HANDLING): 'As a CAA security officer I want classified MOD evidence to be automatically segregated and access-controlled so that security classification is maintained without compromising regulator access. Evidence: MOD autonomy architecture (OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE or SECRET), classified appendices marked per JSP 440, role-based access control enforcing clearance-level visibility.' (3) Define segregation mechanism: system maintains TWO trial case files: [Public Case] (unclassified summary, ConOps outline, non-technical safety case) and [Classified Appendix] (MOD evidence, detailed autonomy logic, cryptographic keys if applicable). Case officer can review both; public can access Public Case only. (4) Add requirement: 'Redaction rules shall be documented so that classifier can justify which evidence is classified and why; applicant can challenge over-redaction.'

findings.finding_title: REQ017 (Public Engagement) and REQ018 (Security Classification) lack user stories and acceptance criteria; no explicit process defined
findings.reasoning: REQ017 and REQ018 were flagged in the raw requirements CSV as "Strongly implied by CAP 1616 and GDPR" and "addresses how classified MOD evidence is presented" respectively, but neither has a user story or acceptance criteria. Grace-goals confirm implicit scope risks: REQ017 "CAP 1616 scope not specified (what level of consultation is sufficient?)" and REQ018 "REQ018 lacks user story and acceptance criteria; no explicit process defined." Yet both are assigned to GOAL-010 (rank 2, conditional) and GOAL-009 (rank 3, optional) respectively, suggesting regulatory importance. Without explicit process definitions, these requirements risk becoming scope creep or being dropped from v1.0.
findings.recommendation: IMMEDIATE: Conduct rapid requirements-gathering sessions with CAA communications (REQ017) and MOD security officer (REQ018) to author explicit user stories and acceptance criteria. GATE: These should not progress to v1.0-minor without signed acceptance criteria. ALTERNATE: If acceptance criteria cannot be defined by Q2 2026, reclassify both as v2.0 (future) and note as out-of-scope for v1.0 panel.

findings.finding_title: REQ002: 'Swarm-aware' and 'defensibly' undefined; no acceptance criteria
findings.original_requirement: As a CAA SORA assessor I want swarm-aware SORA evidence so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly.
findings.verifiable_rewrite: SORA evidence is swarm-aware when it explicitly addresses: (1) Coordinated-link-loss cascades, (2) Emergent misbehavior (deadlock, oscillation), (3) Unintended inter-vehicle interactions. Evidence is defensible when referenced to JARUS SORA v2.5 methodology and documented hazard analysis (FTA or STAMP). Target: >= 3 distinct swarm-aware risk items per aircraft, distributed across 3 categories, with evidence citations.
findings.acceptance_criterion: SORA assessment results in: >= 3 swarm-aware risk items per aircraft, each with evidence citation (test report, FTA, STAMP analysis), all items referenced to JARUS v2.5 methodology, derived SAIL/OSO explicitly reference swarm coordination constraints (e.g., 'Separation >= 50 m horizontal, 20 m vertical during coordinated evasion').
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-1, Dorothy-3: 'swarm-aware' is undefined term (confidence 10/10 criticality); 'defensibly' has no criteria for defense. Chris's definition of 'swarm-aware' is 'methodology or evidence accounting for collective behavior.' Operationalizing through 3-component evidence framework (coordinated-link-loss, emergent-misbehavior, inter-vehicle interactions) makes 'swarm-aware' repeatable. 'Defensibly' tied to JARUS v2.5 + documented analysis.
findings.recommendation: Replace subjective terms ('defensibly', 'swarm-aware') with enumerated criteria and methodology references. Require evidence citations (FTA, STAMP, test reports) to tie defense logic to recognized standards.

findings.finding_title: REQ005: 'Automatic containment' undefined; critical safety gap (5 subcriteria)
findings.original_requirement: As a CAA airspace regulator I want automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS.
findings.verifiable_rewrite: Automatic containment = geofence monitor (onboard, 10 Hz polling) + primary containment action (priority: Return-to-Launch if GPS; fallback: climb-and-hold). Upon geofence breach (position > 10 m beyond boundary for > 2 sec), system triggers containment within 500 ms, logs breach, alerts crew within 1 sec. Design validated through HITL simulation (boundary stress testing with +/- 15 m GPS noise) and flight testing (>= 10 flights, deliberate near-breach scenarios).
findings.acceptance_criterion: 100% simulated geofence-breach scenarios trigger containment within 500 ms; 100% breaches logged (timestamp, coordinates); 100% crew alerts within 1 sec; robust operation confirmed under +/- 15 m GPS uncertainty (worst-case); HITL and flight testing pass (zero uncontained breaches in 10 flights).
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-4 (5 subcriteria undefined for 'containment'); Observation-1: critical safety gap. 'Containment' lacks design and verification criteria. Operationalizing through 3 design components (monitor, action, alert), specific response times (500 ms action, 1 sec alert), and margin testing (15 m GPS noise). Makes containment testable and verifiable.
findings.recommendation: For all safety-critical requirements, specify design components, response-time budgets, margin factors, and verification approach (simulation + flight test). Tie to performance engineering SLAs (e.g., NFR-LAT-005 for decision latency).

findings.finding_title: Scale/target refinements reveal missing requirement specifications for measurable assurance thresholds
findings.requirement: REQ002 (SORA Assessment), REQ007 (DAA Performance), REQ010 (Human Oversight), REQ003 (ConOps Clarity)
findings.source_reference: Vibe QC findings: #1 (SwarmAwareSORAEvidence 15% vs 3-item tolerable), #5 (DAAPerformance 88% vs 99.5%), #3 (MeaningfulHumanControl workload 52/100 vs 70 tolerable), #4 (ConOpsClarity 55% vs 85% tolerable)
findings.reasoning: Vibe analysis establishes measurable targets that the SRS does not contain: (1) SwarmAwareSORAEvidence: Current status 15% of submissions include >3 swarm-specific SORA evidence items; Tolerable threshold is >=3 items per aircraft addressing loss-of-separation, emergent failure, and coordination mechanism. SRS says 'swarm-aware SORA evidence' but does not quantify '3 items minimum' or list the three required categories. (2) DAAPerformance: Current 88% encounter resolution success; Regulatory minimum 99.5% (11.9-point gap). SRS says 'encounters resolved predictably' but does not specify the 99.5% threshold or the DO-365 test scenario library used to measure this. (3) MeaningfulHumanControl: Workload score 52/100 vs tolerable 70. SRS does not quantify workload thresholds or specify NASA-TLX measurement as a gate. (4) ConOpsClarity: Current 55% inter-rater agreement (kappa ~0.55); Tolerable >=0.8 (kappa). SRS does not specify that independent safety reviewers must achieve kappa>=0.8 or what ConOps template sections are mandatory. These gaps mean the workbench cannot operationalize assurance—case officers will lack objective criteria to accept or reject trial evidence.
findings.recommendation: Extract the Vibe scales and targets and integrate them into the SRS as quantified acceptance criteria: REQ002a: 'Each swarm SORA assessment shall contain evidence addressing: (1) Loss-of-separation failure modes, (2) Swarm formation emergent behaviors and failure modes, (3) Command/control dependency and redundancy mechanisms. Minimum 3 evidence items per aircraft.' REQ007a: 'DAA performance shall be validated against the DO-365 encounter scenario library with >= 99.5% resolution success rate.' REQ010a: 'Crew workload during nominal and off-nominal phases shall be measured using NASA-TLX scale with max scores: nominal 60, off-nominal 75, takeover latency <= 2 seconds.' REQ003a: 'ConOps shall include mandatory sections [template] and shall achieve inter-rater agreement (kappa >= 0.8) between two independent safety-case reviewers.' These become measurable acceptance gates.

findings.finding_title: 'Meaningful human control' contested across CAA/MAA/applicant stakeholder groups—no single authoritative definition
findings.requirement: REQ010: Human Oversight and Crew Fitness
findings.source_reference: DeFOSPAM undefined_term finding (meaningful human control, confidence 10); Vibe QC finding #3 (MeaningfulHumanControl 18-point gap, workload bounds deficient); Dorothy notes CAA emphasis on workload/fatigue vs MAA emphasis on override capability vs applicant emphasis on UI clarity
findings.reasoning: REQ010 states 'I want workload duty and take-over evidence so that meaningful human control is demonstrated throughout.' The term 'meaningful human control' appears unverified in glossary (71 unverified of 88 terms). Dorothy (DeFOSPAM) notes this is contested across stakeholders: CAA interprets MHC as low workload (NASA-TLX <60), sustained attention capacity (no fatigue), and situational awareness (crew can articulate swarm state in <5 seconds). MAA (military) interprets it as hardwired override authority + takeover in <2 seconds regardless of autonomy state + crew not dependent on AI decision transparency. Applicants interpret it as intuitive UI + one-button swarm stop + clear alert hierarchy. These three framings are mutually incompatible. Without a single authority, trial evidence packages will be rejected or re-reviewed, delaying authorization.
findings.recommendation: Establish a CAA/MAA/JARUS working group to define MHC operationally. Proposed definition: 'The crew shall demonstrate continuous situational awareness (swarm state articulation in <5 seconds), active monitoring (NASA-TLX workload <60 during nominal ops), override capability (crew-initiated swarm termination in <2 seconds), and takeover readiness (demonstrated through scenario-based simulation and crew fatigue monitoring). Autonomy shall not surprise the crew; if the swarm deviates >10% from ConOps prediction, crew shall be alerted within 5 seconds.' Implement Vibe METER-013 (workload validation in simulation) as a mandatory gate. Publish a CAA exemplar MHC evidence package from a prior UK BVLOS trial.

findings.finding_title: Undefined outcome for incomplete application package in REQ001
findings.original_text: REQ001: 'I want to receive a complete Operational Authorisation pack so that I can open a valid case'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: REQ001 specifies the desired input (complete pack) and outcome (open a valid case) but does NOT specify what happens if pack is incomplete. DeFOSPAM Observation-1 identified this gap. If a pack is incomplete, shall the system: (1) reject the submission and close the case? (2) place the case in 'return-for-clarification' state pending applicant revision? (3) accept the case provisionally and halt at SORA assessment gate? (4) notify CAA case officer to manually decide? Without specifying the outcome, case officers will handle incomplete packs inconsistently, creating confusion for applicants.
findings.proposed_rewrite: REQ001 outcome: Upon submission of incomplete pack (missing one or more mandatory artefacts per Artefact Detector), the system shall: (1) immediately alert applicant with list of missing artefacts and minimum page/content thresholds; (2) place case in 'return-for-clarification' state; (3) set deadline for resubmission (default 14 days); (4) if resubmission deadline is missed, case shall close with status 'withdrawn-incomplete'. Case shall NOT advance to SORA Assessment or payment processing until pack is complete.
findings.reasoning: This is a critical safety and process requirement. Without specifying the outcome for incomplete packs, the trial authorization workflow becomes ambiguous. Applicants may submit incomplete evidence, expecting case officers to manually review and sort; case officers may expect automated rejection. This inconsistency delays trials and erodes regulatory credibility.
findings.recommendation: MAJOR: Add explicit outcome to REQ001 specifying: (1) return-for-clarification state, (2) mandatory artefact list, (3) resubmission deadline (14 days default), (4) closure condition (withdrawal-incomplete). Update Workbench component 1 (Intake) to implement automated detection and notification.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'emergent behaviour' lacks specification of desired vs. undesired and verification method
findings.original_text: REQ008: 'I want the decision-making architecture airborne limits and emergent-behaviour evidence so that I can assure what the swarm will do collectively'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: Chris's language file defines emergent behavior as 'Behavior arising from collective swarm interactions not explicitly programmed in individual aircraft. Can be desired (formation cohesion) or undesired (deadlock, separation loss).' But REQ008 does not specify which emergent behaviors are acceptable vs. which must be prevented. Is deadlock acceptable if it occurs in < 0.1% of scenarios? Is separation loss < 50 meters acceptable during evasion? Without specifying acceptance criteria and verification method, different implementers will have different safety thresholds for emergent behavior.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Emergent-behaviour evidence shall demonstrate: (1) identification of all potential emergent behaviors (desired and undesired) arising from swarm decision-making architecture and inter-vehicle interactions; (2) formal verification or Monte Carlo simulation bounding the probability of undesired emergent behaviors: formation deadlock < 0.1% probability, separation loss > 100 meters < 0.01% probability, leader loss cascade < 0.01% probability; (3) HITL testing demonstrating swarm behavior variance across environmental noise and sensor uncertainty; (4) documented recovery procedures if undesired emergent behavior is detected during trial. Undesired emergent behaviors that cannot be bounded to acceptable levels shall be grounds for trial rejection or phase suspension.
findings.reasoning: Emergent behavior is inherently hard to predict in multi-agent systems. Regulators need assurance that undesired emergent behaviors are either provably absent or bounded to acceptable probability. Without specifying which behaviors are undesired and what probability bounds are acceptable, trial assurance becomes subjective. Different regulators may have different thresholds.
findings.recommendation: MAJOR: Define 'emergent-behaviour' in SRS glossary with enumeration of: (1) desired behaviors (formation cohesion, coordinated evasion), (2) undesired behaviors (deadlock, separation loss, Byzantine cascades), (3) acceptance criteria (probability bounds per behavior type). Update REQ008 to reference these definitions and require formal verification or Monte Carlo bounding.

findings.finding_title: Undefined term 'independent termination' lacks trigger mechanism and response time
findings.original_text: REQ011: 'I want independent termination a contingency matrix and ATSU coordination so that the trial can be made safe'
findings.ambiguity_explanation: DeFOSPAM identified 'independent termination' as undefined on mechanism and time-to-effect. What does 'independent' mean? Independent of: (1) C2 link (can work if C2 is lost)? (2) onboard autonomy (can work if autonomy fails)? (3) both? Does termination mean: (1) engine shutdown? (2) parachute deployment? (3) both as redundancy? What triggers termination: (1) geofence breach (automatic)? (2) crew command (RF kill-switch)? (3) both? How fast: (1) 100 ms? (2) 500 ms? (3) 2 seconds? Without specifying these details, different implementations may have vastly different safety profiles.
findings.proposed_rewrite: Independent Flight Termination (IFT) shall function independent of C2 link, inter-vehicle links, and onboard autonomy. System shall include: (1) primary trigger (crew-operated RF kill-switch on ground), (2) secondary trigger (onboard geofence breach, automatic), (3) mechanism (engine cutoff within 500 milliseconds, or parachute deployment if equipped). IFT shall be fail-safe: absence of command to extend flight shall result in termination. IFT shall be validated through: (1) ground testing (RF range testing, geofence trigger testing), (2) flight test (actual termination during controlled descent to landing). Upon IFT activation, crew shall immediately notify ATSU and recover aircraft and evidence.
findings.reasoning: Flight termination is a critical safety system. 'Independent termination' is a high-stakes requirement that must specify mechanism and response time. DeFOSPAM identified this as missing outcome (Observation-4) because the requirement lacks actionable detail.
findings.recommendation: MAJOR: Define 'independent termination' operationally. Specify: (1) independence criteria (C2-independent, autonomy-independent), (2) mechanism (engine cutoff or parachute), (3) trigger (crew RF kill-switch or automatic geofence), (4) response time (< 500 milliseconds), (5) verification (ground test + flight test). Update REQ011 to reference this definition.

findings.finding_title: Emergent-behavior credibility boundary undefined; no accepted verification standard for multi-agent systems
findings.gap_description: REQ008 (Swarm Decision-Making Architecture Review) requires 'emergent-behavior evidence' bounded through formal verification (model checking) and Monte Carlo simulation (1,000 trials, <0.1% separation-loss rate). But credibility boundary undefined: unclear what separation-loss rate (0.1%? 0.01%?) is acceptable, and no accepted standard exists for verifying emergent behavior in multi-agent autonomous systems. Formal verification tools for swarm systems are immature; Monte Carlo sample-size justification missing.

tbd_details.reason: Formal methods for multi-agent systems are active research area; no mature verification standard yet exists for civil-aviation autonomy. 0.1% threshold is heuristic.
tbd_details.responsible: CAA Autonomy Review Panel + academic consensus group (formal-methods researchers, complexity scientists, multi-agent systems domain experts)
tbd_details.blocking_path: Phase 1-2 trials do not require swarm emergent-behavior verification (single or dual aircraft). Phase 3 swarm trials cannot proceed without CAA-accepted emergent-behavior evidence standard.

findings.reasoning: Odin REQ008 rewrite specifies formal verification + Monte Carlo approach but lacks justification for credibility thresholds. Grace goal GOAL-002 (Assure Swarm-Aware Safety Assessment) includes 'SwarmEmergentBoundedness' scale but the measurement methodology is incomplete. Risk: applicant submits Monte Carlo simulation results (1,000 trials, 0.09% separation loss) claiming success; CAA reviewer cannot determine if 0.09% is acceptable without published guidance.
findings.recommendation: Establish emergent-behavior assurance working group (Q3 2026): CAA + formal-methods research labs (University College London, Imperial College) + complexity scientists. Develop: (1) failure-mode taxonomy for emergent swarm behavior (deadlock, oscillation, separation-loss cascades, divergence from ConOps); (2) formal-verification approach (model checking vs. theorem proving vs. runtime monitoring) per failure-mode type; (3) Monte Carlo validation methodology (scenario library, sample-size calculation, statistical significance testing); (4) acceptance criteria per failure mode (e.g., 'deadlock must not occur in >99.99% of trials; separation-loss cascade depth must not exceed 3 aircraft'). Publish 'Emergent-Behavior Assurance Guidance' by end Q4 2026.

findings.finding_title: REQ006 (C2 Link Survivability) vs REQ007 (DAA Predictable Resolution) - Logical Conflict on Link Loss
findings.conflict_description: REQ006 requires C2 link survivability (swarm operates if C2 is lost). REQ007 requires DAA encounters are resolved predictably (avoidance decisions are consistent). If C2 is lost during an encounter, who decides avoidance? The requirements do not specify whether DAA continues operating under inter-vehicle consensus or pauses pending C2 recovery. These create conflicting operational scenarios.
findings.reasoning: REQ006 user story: 'authorised monitored C2 and inter-vehicle links so that link loss is survivable.' This implies swarm continues functioning without C2 (autonomous contingency). REQ007 user story: 'DO-365 / F3442-aligned evidence for each aircraft and coordinated swarm de-confliction so that cooperative and non-cooperative encounters are resolved predictably.' This requires coordinated de-confliction, which assumes communication is available. If C2 fails during a non-cooperative encounter (per REQ007 worst-case), how is encounter resolution predictable? Paul's findings flag this: 'DAA non-cooperative encounter resolution action completely unspecified.' The coordination mechanism for REQ007 is not specified; if inter-vehicle links are the coordination mechanism, and those links also fail (REQ006 scenario), DAA coordination breaks. Conflict: REQ006 assumes survivability without C2; REQ007 assumes coordination during encounter.
findings.recommendation: Define explicit encounter handling under link loss: (1) REQ007 requires two encounter-resolution modes: (a) COORDINATED (C2 and inter-vehicle links available): swarm members coordinate avoidance via inter-vehicle broadcast and consensus; (b) AUTONOMOUS (C2 lost but inter-vehicle available): all members execute identical conservative avoidance (climb +300ft) without coordination overhead. (2) REQ006 requires inter-vehicle link redundancy: if primary C2 fails, inter-vehicle RF continues (defines backup frequency, range, redundancy N). (3) Add requirement: 'If both C2 and inter-vehicle links fail simultaneously: (a) each swarm member executes pre-planned encounter avoidance autonomously (not coordinated); (b) each member broadcasts 'LINK LOSS' status via emergency beacon; (c) crew is alerted 'CRITICAL LINK LOSS - ALL COMMS DOWN'; (d) system initiates 60-second autonomous RTL if crew cannot restore links; (e) after 60s, independent termination activates. Define link-loss detection latency (<1s), RTL route, and termination triggers.'

findings.finding_title: REQ010: 'Meaningful human control' undefined; 'throughout' temporal ambiguity; workload thresholds missing
findings.original_requirement: As a CAA human factors inspector I want workload duty and take-over evidence so that meaningful human control is demonstrated throughout.
findings.verifiable_rewrite: 'Meaningful human control' = (1) continuous crew situational awareness (>= 1 Hz updates per aircraft), (2) retained authority (override latency < 2 sec), (3) intervention capability (time window >= 5-10 sec for evasion/return-to-launch). Workload quantified per flight phase (nominal <= 75% cognitive capacity, contingency <= 85%). Takeover demonstrated through: (A) time-to-takeover (VLOS <= 5 sec, BVLOS single <= 10 sec, BVLOS swarm <= 15 sec), (B) >= 10 simulator takeover sessions per crew with instructor sign-off, (C) full 6-DOF control authority upon takeover.
findings.acceptance_criterion: Crew workload (NASA-TLX) <= 75% nominal, <= 85% contingency in 100% of test flights; situational awareness >= 1 Hz for 100% of duration; override latency < 2 sec for 100% of commands; time-to-takeover within phase limits (VLOS <= 5, BVLOS single <= 10, BVLOS swarm <= 15 sec) for 100% of takeovers; >= 10 documented simulator sessions per crew with instructor sign-off.
findings.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Dorothy-2: 'meaningful human control' lacks measurable criteria. 'Throughout' is temporal ambiguity (no assessment frequency). Workload and takeover thresholds undefined. Chris defines 'meaningful' as 'Active, informed crew awareness and retained authority.' Operationalizing through: (1) frequency metric (1 Hz), (2) override latency (< 2 sec), (3) intervention time window (5-10 sec phase-dependent), (4) workload thresholds (75-85%), (5) measurement (NASA-TLX, simulator, observer). 'Throughout' resolved through per-phase acceptance.
findings.recommendation: For all human-factors requirements, specify: (1) awareness frequency (Hz), (2) authority latency (milliseconds), (3) intervention time windows (seconds, phase-dependent), (4) workload thresholds (% of cognitive capacity), (5) measurement instrument (NASA-TLX, SWAT, etc.). Require simulator practice hours and flight-test observer validation.

findings.finding_title: Terminology Collision: 'Complete' Used with Conflicting Meanings Across Requirements
findings.reasoning: REQ001 (Application Intake & Completeness Check) uses 'complete' to mean 'all mandatory fields submitted and valid'. REQ012 (Data MOR & DPIA Obligations) uses 'complete' to mean 'satisfies GDPR Article 35 Data Protection Impact Assessment requirements'. These two meanings are semantically distinct but both occupy the glossary term 'Completeness' without disambiguation. Chelcie-findings.json (finding-003) and odin-findings.json (finding-009) both flagged this collision. When SRS reviewers or implementers reference 'complete', ambiguity arises: does a pack with all fields submitted satisfy both REQ001 and REQ012, or are these independent completeness gates? This creates risk of missed validation gates in implementer code.
findings.recommendation: Establish distinct glossary terms: (1) 'Application Pack Completeness' for REQ001 (all mandatory fields present and syntactically valid), (2) 'GDPR Completeness' for REQ012 (DPIA requirements satisfied per Art. 35). Update all cross-references and child FRs (FR-001 through FR-007 for REQ001; FR-221-260 for REQ012) to use precise terminology. Add decision rule in Section 3.2 (Specific Requirements Structure) clarifying that these gates are sequential: Application Pack Completeness is prerequisite; GDPR Completeness is applied conditionally if pack is PII-intensive.

findings.finding_title: REQ016 (Phased Trial Build-Up) is a single point of failure affecting 5+ dependent requirements
